title: The Eye and the Heart
summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00095, a keyed encoding of Eleanor Prescott Hammond's complete 1911 Anglia article. Hammond directly examined and copied the anonymous Middle English translation from Longleat MS 258, ff. 102–119, and compared it with French texts printed by Thomas Wright and in Le Jardin de Plaisance. The anonymous English work translates Michault Taillevent's French Debat du coeur et de l'oeil of c. 1444 and also survives in an undated Wynkyn de Worde print conventionally dated c. 1516. Current evidence does not show that de Worde printed from Longleat: the manuscript's printer's marks do not match the surviving print and may indicate a lost second printing. Longleat reports that MS 258 was fully digitised, but only a selected public image set was found; whole-object access and reuse terms remain unresolved.
review_notes: The graph distinguishes the complete Hammond article, the abstract English translation, its manuscript and printed witnesses, the French source work and representative French witness, modern digital encodings, and reproduction objects.

A complete public whole-manuscript facsimile of Longleat MS 258 was not found. The cache preserves every Longleat manuscript still image published on the official exhibition page: eight JPEGs in one auditable bundle plus the ninth PNG detail, with only ff. 107r and 108r overlapping the poem.
Public access and local research caching do not establish redistribution rights. Longleat's current request form limits supplied research images and requires separate written reproduction permission.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00095
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The source submodule is a separate repository and was not modified during this research.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00095
entities.description: A publicly distributed keyed and structurally marked-up electronic text of Hammond's article, including modern prose, the Middle English poem, notes, page breaks, and editorial italics.

entities.label: Hammond 1911 article and Longleat edition
entities.description: The complete article represented by CME00095: a seven-paragraph introduction, Hammond's complete transcription and annotation of the Longleat poem, a manuscript-ending note, and an afterword.

bibliographic.citation: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, ‘The Eye and the Heart,’ Anglia: Zeitschrift für englische Philologie 34, new series 22 (1911): 235–265.
bibliographic.title: The Eye and the Heart

notes: The complete-volume title page confirms Halle a. S., Max Niemeyer, 1911, Band XXXIV / Neue Folge XXII; Internet Archive serial metadata incorrectly exposes the inherited serial start date 1877.
Printed p. 234 precedes the article and p. 266 begins the next article, confirming the complete boundary.

entities.label: The Eye and the Heart
alternate_labels: A Lytel Treatyse called the Dysputacyon or Complaynt of the Heart thorughe perced with the Lokynge of the Eye
In the first week of the season of May
entities.description: The anonymous Middle English translation/version as an abstract work: 103 eight-line stanzas and 824 lines in ababbcbc rhyme, distinct from Hammond's article and from either surviving witness.
notes: DIMEV records one manuscript witness and one early printed witness.
The English translator is anonymous; Michault Taillevent is author of the French source work, not the English translator.

entities.label: Longleat House, MS 258
entities.description: The direct manuscript source of Hammond's English text. The Eye and the Heart occupies ff. 102–119, begins without a heading with a four-line capital, and ends without an explicit.

holding.notes: No repository-owned exact item catalog page was found; Longleat's current access page and image-request form provide the institutional access routes.

physical_description.support: Outer sheet of each gathering vellum and three inner sheets paper
physical_description.extent: 147 leaves; the fifth gathering is lost
physical_description.format: Modern-bound verse miscellany, gatherings chiefly of eight
notes: Hammond describes almost all of the manuscript as the work of one small, legible, current but inelegant hand, generally writing six stanzas per leaf.
Later running titles, corrections, ownership marks, and printer's annotations occur; ‘Constat John Thynne’ appears on the opening page.
Hammond uses legacy recto/verso notation ‘102a’ and ‘119a’; DIMEV records ff. 102–119. The current exhibition labels rectos as 107r and 108r.
The c. 1475–1500 copying date is not the English translation's exact composition date.
Hammond's statement that the poem was unique in manuscript remains compatible with DIMEV's separate early print witness.

entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde print of The Eye and the Heart, STC 6915
alternate_labels: A lytell treatyse called the dysputacyon or the complaynte of the herte through perced with the lokyng of the eye
entities.description: A separate early printed witness, conventionally dated [1516?]. The official Longleat exhibition reproduces opening leaf Ai from the Huntington Library copy.

bibliographic.citation: A lytell treatyse called the dysputacyon or the complaynte of the herte through perced with the lokyng of the eye (London: Wynkyn de Worde, [1516?]), STC 6915.
bibliographic.title: A lytell treatyse called the dysputacyon or the complaynte of the herte through perced with the lokyng of the eye

holding.notes: The exhibition identifies the image source as the Huntington Library but does not give the copy's shelfmark.

notes: No direct copy relationship from Longleat is asserted. The exhibition states that de Worde's layout does not match the manuscript's printer's marks and suggests that a second, now-lost print may once have existed.

entities.label: Debat du coeur et de l'oeil
alternate_labels: Le Debat du cuer et de l'oeil
entities.description: Michault Taillevent's French love debate, composed c. 1444: 824 octosyllabic lines in 103 eight-line stanzas rhyming ababbcbc. It is the source work translated into Middle English, not necessarily the translator's surviving physical exemplar.
notes: JONAS records a broad manuscript tradition; British Library Royal MS 19 A III is one witness, not the sole French witness.

entities.label: British Library, Royal MS 19 A III
entities.description: A French manuscript witness containing Michault Taillevent's Debat du coeur et de l'oeil at ff. 29r–41v. Thomas Wright published this witness in 1841, and Hammond used Wright's text for detailed comparison.

holding.notes: Biblissima supplies the exact component mapping; no current repository-owned exact item page for this shelfmark was located during review.

notes: Hammond reports that the French leaves were disordered by binding and gives a stanza sequence for reconstructing reading order.
This is a source for Wright's edition and Hammond's comparison, not an established physical exemplar of the anonymous English translator.

entities.label: Thomas Wright's 1841 French edition
entities.description: Wright's printing of Le Debat du Cuer et de l'Oeil from the Paris manuscript then designated Bibliothèque du Roi 7388, embedded in a larger Camden Society volume.

bibliographic.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., The Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes, Camden Society, original series 16 (London: J. B. Nichols and Son, 1841), 310–321.
bibliographic.title: The Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes

notes: The printed French poem begins on p. 310 and ends on p. 321; Hammond's ‘pp. 310 ff.’ is not a claim that the work ends at p. 318.

entities.label: Le Jardin de plaisance 1910 facsimile
entities.description: The Société des anciens textes français facsimile of Antoine Vérard's circa-1501 anthology, including a correctly ordered printing of the French Debat du coeur et de l'oeil.

bibliographic.citation: Le Jardin de plaisance et fleur de rhétorique, facsimile of the edition printed by Antoine Vérard c. 1501, ed. Eugénie Droz and Arthur Piaget (Paris: Firmin-Didot for the Société des anciens textes français, 1910).
bibliographic.title: Le Jardin de plaisance et fleur de rhétorique

notes: The cached complete-volume PDF contains the French debate on digital PDF pages 122–129; the exact original leaf signatures remain to be reconciled.

entities.label: Hammond 1905 description of Longleat MS 258
entities.description: Hammond's earlier three-page codicological note, which the 1911 article explicitly summarizes.

bibliographic.citation: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, ‘MS. Longleat 258—A Chaucerian Codex,’ Modern Language Notes 20.3 (March 1905): 77–79.
bibliographic.title: MS. Longleat 258—A Chaucerian Codex

access.contact: collections@longleat.co.uk; Longleat House Collections, The Estate Office, Warminster, Wiltshire BA12 7NW, United Kingdom
access.format: JPG digital photographs, scanned microfilm PDF, or quoted multi-image order
notes: Current form prices: £30 + VAT for the first digital photograph from one volume and £5 + VAT per subsequent image; multiple-volume requests are quoted; scanned microfilm PDF is £45 + VAT.

The form limits a research request to ten images and requires a separate quote/arrangement for broader delivery; a complete 147-leaf color surrogate is therefore not priced by the simple per-image line alone.
A mediated whole-object request should explicitly include covers/boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and every manuscript leaf.

access.access_method: Arrange an advanced reader appointment with Longleat Collections; manuscript consultation requires a research need, registration, identification, and a letter of introduction.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The Eye and the Heart occupies one complete manuscript run in Longleat MS 258, ff. 102–119.
editorial_practices.representation: Hammond prints all 103 stanzas, adds a supplied title and numbered stanza headings, and records the unheaded opening and blank manuscript ending.
editorial_practices.description: The article is complete and the manuscript witness is transcribed in full, but the article is not a facsimile and does not reproduce the rest of the codex.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Wright's disordered French manuscript text and a correctly ordered circa-1501 French printing
editorial_practices.representation: Hammond provides a corrected stanza order, quotes French readings in notes, and distinguishes translator padding from errors of the immediate Longleat scribe.
editorial_practices.description: French comparison is editorial evidence; neither surviving French source object is asserted as the anonymous translator's physical exemplar.
editorial_practices.scope: Introduction, notes to the English poem, and afterword

editorial_practices.source_feature: Longleat abbreviation marks and uncertain or supplied manuscript letters
editorial_practices.representation: Many short letter sequences appear in italics inside words, and CME preserves those source italics as HI REND='italic'.
editorial_practices.description: The typography strongly functions as editorial expansion marking, but Hammond does not supply a global method statement and only two target leaves are publicly pictured. The exact sign-by-sign relation between manuscript marks and italics remains unresolved.
editorial_practices.scope: Middle English poem, especially embedded italic letter sequences

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete article with prose framing, 103 numbered stanzas, notes, page breaks, line-number milestones, and a tailnote
editorial_practices.representation: Three DIV1 elements encode introduction, poem, and afterword; 103 LG and 824 L elements encode the poem; 56 NOTE, 31 PB, and 164 MILESTONE elements preserve editorial apparatus and navigation.
editorial_practices.description: The encoding represents the complete article structure, not simply a free-standing medieval poem; XML node counts are representation metrics.

evidence.citation: CME00095 XML in the pinned CME submodule, structurally inspected 2026-07-13.
evidence.locator: TEMPHEAD/REVDESCR; BODY/DIV1 introduction, poem, and afterword; LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, TAILNOTE, CLOSER
evidence.summary: Confirms identifiers, complete article structure, 103 stanza groups and 824 lines, modern editorial framing, page/line milestones, Longleat folio notes, typographic italics, afterword, and Hammond signature.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English item CME00095 and current Middle English Compendium description.
evidence.summary: Identifies the stable electronic-text item and U-M's description of the CME as re-keyed, structurally marked-up public-domain editions distributed without fee or restriction.
notes: The exact item page returned an automated-access challenge during this review; the stable resolver, repository identifiers, and collection description remain consistent.

evidence.citation: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, ‘The Eye and the Heart,’ Anglia 34, new series 22 (1911): 235–265; complete-volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete article, direct Longleat examination and copying, codex description, precise poem loci and boundary, French and early-print comparisons, complete annotated poem, and afterword.

evidence.citation: Direct title-page, boundary, pagination, OCR, and metadata audit of Internet Archive item angliazeitschrif34tbuoft.
evidence.locator: volume title page; PDF pp. 244–276; 350-page file; cached OCR and metadata
evidence.summary: Confirms the 1911 volume imprint, article start and end, complete carrier-volume coverage, and the conflict between the volume title page and inherited serial metadata year 1877.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse v1.0, record 2603 (IMEV 1548; NIMEV TM 791 | TP 918), linked witness, printed-book, and bibliography records.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-2603; Manuscripts.xml Longleat258 and BLRoy19Aiii; PrintedBooks.xml STC6915; Bibliography.xml Hammond1911b and Wright1841b
evidence.summary: Supplies the English work identifiers, 103 eight-line ababbcbc stanzas, debate and love-court subjects, Longleat ff. 102–119, separate STC 6915 witness, French source references, and edition citations; bundled license is CC BY 4.0.

evidence.citation: Longleat House Collections and University of Birmingham, ‘Medieval Love Debate at Longleat.’
evidence.summary: Dates MS 258 c. 1475–1500, identifies the English poem and French source, states that MS 258 was fully digitised, presents selected manuscript and print images, explains the nonmatching printer's marks, and states image copyright and reuse contact information.

evidence.citation: Inventory and integrity audit of every Longleat MS 258 still image published on the official exhibition page.
evidence.locator: eight JPEG inventory entries and one separately cached PNG; poem overlap ff. 107r and 108r
evidence.summary: Confirms successful capture and ZIP integrity for all eight JPEGs, exact URLs and checksums, the ninth PNG detail, and that the public selection is partial relative to both manuscript and poem.

evidence.citation: Longleat House Collections, ‘Access the Collections,’ and current Image and Video Request Form.
evidence.summary: Confirms mediated manuscript and reading-room access, availability of certain manuscript microfilm PDFs, current research-image and microfilm fees, collections@longleat.co.uk, research-only supply terms, and separate reproduction-license requirements.

evidence.citation: Direct inspection of the Longleat exhibition's Huntington Library image of Wynkyn de Worde STC 6915, opening leaf Ai.
evidence.summary: Visually confirms the printed title and woodcut on the one publicly supplied opening leaf; it does not establish complete-print coverage or a Longleat-to-de Worde copy relationship.

evidence.citation: Eleanor Prescott Hammond, ‘MS. Longleat 258—A Chaucerian Codex,’ Modern Language Notes 20.3 (March 1905): 77–79.
evidence.summary: Provides the earlier codicological description that Hammond explicitly summarizes in her 1911 article.

evidence.citation: Cécile Bruneau, record for Michault Taillevent's Debat du coeur et de l'oeil, JONAS, IRHT/CNRS.
evidence.locator: validated work record; date, language, versification, and manuscript witness list
evidence.summary: Attributes the French work to Michault Taillevent, dates it c. 1444, and describes 824 octosyllabic lines in 103 ababbcbc stanzas across a broad manuscript tradition.

evidence.citation: Biblissima aggregation for London, British Library, Royal MS 19 A III.
evidence.locator: Michault Taillevent, Debat du coeur et de l'oeil, ff. 29r–41v
evidence.summary: Identifies the current British Library shelfmark, author/title association, and exact French-work folios.
notes: Biblissima is an aggregator rather than the holding institution; no current BL exact-item page was found.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, ed., The Latin Poems Commonly Attributed to Walter Mapes (1841), 310–321; complete-volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms Wright's complete French printing, its source statement ‘From the Bibl. du Roi, at Paris, MS. 7388,’ and the exact printed extent consulted by Hammond.

evidence.citation: Le Jardin de plaisance et fleur de rhétorique, 1910 facsimile of Antoine Vérard's circa-1501 edition; complete-volume scan.
evidence.summary: Confirms the complete facsimile volume and the correctly ordered French debate cited by Hammond; exact original leaf signatures remain to be reconciled.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation Services and Imaging Services terms.
evidence.summary: Provides a current order route, required shelfmark and folio information, color-request guidance, reference/reproduction options, image-use warnings, and licensing contact; current digitised-manuscripts pages do not expose a verified Royal MS 19 A III whole-object viewer.

open_questions.question: Will Longleat supply a complete color digital facsimile of MS 258, including exterior and endmatter surfaces, and what public-repository or publication reuse will it license?
next_steps: If a request is made, record Longleat's reply, quote, deliverable format, physical coverage, and exact license terms.
Request covers/boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, edges if offered, and all 147 manuscript leaves; map The Eye and the Heart separately to ff. 102–119.
Do not commit or redistribute supplied files unless written authorization covers the Git repository and any commercial print-on-demand use.
notes: Longleat's official access page and current image-request form provide the identified request and licensing route; no complete facsimile was consulted for this record.

open_questions.question: Which printing, if any, was prepared from Longleat MS 258's printer-marked leaves?
next_steps: Compare all printer marks throughout a complete MS 258 facsimile with the full STC 6915 layout.
Consult current bibliographical scholarship for evidence of a lost second printing.
Retain Longleat and STC 6915 as parallel witnesses until direct copying evidence emerges.

open_questions.question: What is the Huntington shelfmark and can a complete scholarly facsimile of STC 6915 be viewed or purchased?
next_steps: Ask Huntington reference staff to identify the exact copy reproduced by Longleat and its complete-image availability.
Record any ESTC/USTC copy record and distinguish catalog access from image access.

open_questions.question: Does British Library Royal MS 19 A III have a recoverable complete digital object, or must images be ordered through Digitisation Services?
next_steps: Ask the British Library whether a legacy or restored complete digital object exists.
If ordering, request the whole manuscript for source context where feasible and map the French work to ff. 29r–41v.

open_questions.question: What French exemplar or branch underlies the anonymous English translation, and can its translation date and place be narrowed?
next_steps: Consult a modern critical edition and stemmatic study of the French and English versions.
Do not promote Royal MS 19 A III or the circa-1501 print from Hammond's comparison layer into the anonymous translator's physical source chain without evidence.

open_questions.question: Which Hammond/CME italics map to abbreviation signs, supplied letters, or other features in Longleat MS 258?
next_steps: Request or obtain a complete manuscript facsimile and compare every italic span with the manuscript when available.
Document a sign-by-sign expansion policy and preserve uncertainty rather than treating italics as emphasis.
